critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

The sepsis model paper's main claim — that the model improved early sepsis recognition and downstream care — is not supported by the presented design. The concurrent introduction of a nurse-driven sepsis bundle and evening staffing increase are the key confounders. These changes directly accelerate sepsis treatment initiation, which is the mechanism by which ICU transfers would be reduced. The before-after comparison at a single site without any contemporaneous control (Hospital B was excluded) cannot disentangle model effects from these co-interventions.

The model's retrospective AUROC is good evidence of predictive validity. Clinician uptake of top-decile alerts is an operational credibility signal. Neither supports the causal outcome claim.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed — the model is a credible predictive tool; its causal contribution to outcomes requires a controlled study design such as staggered rollout, stepped-wedge, or difference-in-differences with a non-deploying control hospital.
